page_full_text:
B. Retrieval Strategy Taxonomy
Retrieval strategy taxonomy captures when and how the
Retriever is invoked across a trajectory, and how retrieved
evidence is incorporated into dynamic context construction.
Agentic systems increasingly treat retrieval as a repeated, state-
dependent action rather than an upfront preprocessing step [7],
[40], [41].
1) One-Shot Retrieval: One-Shot Retrieval refers to a sin-
gle retrieval action conditioned on the user query followed by
generation conditioned on a fixed retrieved context, matching
baseline RAG [1], [40]. Within Agentic RAG, this remains
a class where no state-dependent retrieval actions occur after
initiation, regardless of whether a Planner exists [45], [46].
2) Iterative Retrieval: Iterative Retrieval performs multiple
retrieval actions during a single query resolution, where later
retrievals depend on intermediate state. IRCoT interleaves
retrieval with Chain-of-Thought steps [9]. Iter-RetGen repeats
retrieval and generation with intermediate generations inform-
ing retrieval [5]. This class increases the degrees of freedom
of the control policy and tightly couples retrieval with token
economics [42].
3) Self-Refining Retrieval: Self-Refining Retrieval couples
retrieval with critique, revision, or self-evaluation such that
queries and evidence are refined to increase faithfulness [6],
[57]. Self-RAG learns to retrieve on-demand and critique
both retrieved passages and generations [6]. Such systems
often employ hybrid or learned control policies to drive active
knowledge assimilation from retrieved evidence [58], [59].

C. Reasoning Taxonomy
Reasoning taxonomy classifies the form of multi-step rea-
soning used to decide tool invocation and transform evidence
Fig. 3. Taxonomy of Agentic RAG systems across architecture, retrieval
into grounded outputs. We adopt four classes: Chain-of-
strategy, reasoning paradigm, and memory/context management. This struc-
Thought, ReAct-style interleaving, reflection-based reasoning,
tural mapping demonstrates how orthogonal control-flow decisions combine
to form distinct, reproducible agentic archetypes.
and tree-based exploration [12], [13], [23], [30].
1) Chain-of-Thought & ReAct-Style: Chain-of-Thought
(CoT) prompting elicits a sequential reasoning trace of inter-
2) Planner–Executor Architectures: Planner–Executor ar- mediate steps [23], frequently acting as a query-construction
chitectures separate the Planner (which decomposes goals, substrate in IRCoT and planning decompositions [9], [28].
selects tool invocation, and sets retrieval objectives) from anReAct extends this by interleaving reasoning steps with actions
Executor (which carries out retrieval and returns observations).(tool invocations), producing observations that update subse-
The defining criterion is explicit role separation and an inter-quent reasoning [12]. Many agent frameworks describe agents
role interface that mediates decision and action [11], [50],as running tools in a loop until a stop condition, corresponding
[51]. HuggingGPT adopts a controller/executor framing whereclosely to the ReAct taxonomy class [45], [46].
an LLM orchestrates specialized models, while tool-use docu-
2) Reflection & Tree-Based Exploration: Reflection-based
mentation highlights that tool calling is a multi-step interaction
reasoning introduces explicit self-evaluation steps that critique
boundary requiring delegation and handoffs [42], [43].
intermediate reasoning, retrieved evidence, or generated asser-
3) Multi-Agent RAG Systems: Multi-Agent RAG Systemstions. Reflexion stores this feedback in an episodic memory
distribute planning, retrieval, and generation across multiplebuffer to improve later behavior [13], while RARR retrieves
agents that interact to complete a query. The defining propertyevidence specifically to attribute and revise generated text
is distributed decision-making with interaction among agents[57]. Conversely, Tree-based exploration treats reasoning as
[44], [52]–[54]. AutoGen formalizes multi-agent conversa- a search over multiple candidate branches. Tree-of-Thoughts
tion with tool-using agents [52], [55], whereas frameworksrealizes this by proposing, evaluating, and expanding thoughts
like LangGraph provide an orchestration substrate for graph-with backtracking [30], supporting evidence gathering for
structured agentic workloads [53], [56].
competing hypotheses.

B. Retrieval Strategy Taxonomy

Retrieval strategy taxonomy captures when and how theRetriever is invoked across a trajectory, and how retrievedevidence is incorporated into dynamic context construction.Agentic systems increasingly treat retrieval as a repeated, state-dependent action rather than an upfront preprocessing step [7],[40], [41].         1) One-Shot Retrieval: One-Shot Retrieval refers to a sin-gle retrieval action conditioned on the user query followed bygeneration conditioned on a fixed retrieved context, matchingbaseline RAG [1], [40]. Within Agentic RAG, this remainsa class where no state-dependent retrieval actions occur afterinitiation, regardless of whether a Planner exists [45], [46].2) Iterative Retrieval: Iterative Retrieval performs multipleretrieval actions during a single query resolution, where laterretrievals depend on intermediate state. IRCoT interleavesretrieval with Chain-of-Thought steps [9]. Iter-RetGen repeatsretrieval and generation with intermediate generations inform-ing retrieval [5]. This class increases the degrees of freedomof the control policy and tightly couples retrieval with tokeneconomics [42].         3) Self-Refining Retrieval: Self-Refining Retrieval couplesretrieval with critique, revision, or self-evaluation such thatqueries and evidence are refined to increase faithfulness [6],[57]. Self-RAG learns to retrieve on-demand and critiqueboth retrieved passages and generations [6]. Such systemsoften employ hybrid or learned control policies to drive activeknowledge assimilation from retrieved evidence [58], [59].
